refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit connection to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, optimization logic, and operator awareness. This supports both direct operation and teleoperation modes.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 8-hour shift, multi-day project phase). Compare against E_target. Verify that the NGHE solution provides the necessary telemetry, control authority, and feedback to enable this measurement and optimization.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and report energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tonnage moved) to enable real-time and post-hoc energy efficiency calculation.

CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide control mechanisms (power management, load optimization, operational mode selection) that allow operators and tele-operators to influence energy consumption during task execution.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall deliver real-time energy efficiency feedback (energy_per_ton KPI, efficiency alerts, optimization recommendations) to operators and tele-operators to support decision-making.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain energy efficiency performance within safety and operational constraints (worksite safety plan, equipment limits, task requirements).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that monitors power draw across all vehicle subsystems, calculates energy_per_ton in real-time, and provides optimization recommendations via telemetry to both direct and remote operators.

DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring and efficiency calculation across vehicle control modules, with aggregation and reporting via a teleoperation interface; operators manually adjust operational parameters based on periodic efficiency reports.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle-resident energy monitoring and control optimization (DP-A core logic) with safety-critical efficiency constraints enforced by Safety Services; teleoperation layer provides operator feedback and mode selection authority.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid centralized-distributed energy management with safety integration. Vehicle hosts real-time energy monitoring, efficiency calculation, and control optimization logic. Safety Services enforces energy-efficiency-related safety constraints (e.g., preventing unsafe power-saving modes, ensuring load limits). Teleoperation layer delivers operator feedback (Telemetry interface: energy_per_ton, efficiency alerts) and accepts operator control inputs (Tasking interface: operational mode, load strategy). This design balances real-time responsiveness (vehicle-resident logic), safety assurance (Safety Services integration), and operator authority (teleoperation feedback loop).

potential_sources: Vehicle energy monitoring logic is coupled to vehicle control subsystems (motor, hydraulics, transmission) — acceptable; these are internal to Vehicle module.

Safety Services must couple to Vehicle efficiency constraints — acceptable; this is a safety-critical interface with clear ownership (Safety Services owns constraint definition, Vehicle owns enforcement).
Teleoperation must couple to Vehicle telemetry and tasking — acceptable; these are well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C avoids problematic coupling: does not require Teleoperation to perform real-time efficiency calculations (latency risk), does not isolate energy optimization from safety constraints, and does not duplicate monitoring logic across modules.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle subsystems (motor, hydraulics, transmission) provide measurable power draw signals or can be instrumented to do so.
ASSUMPTION: E_target is defined by customer and provided as a configuration parameter to the NGHE solution.
RISK: If vehicle power draw signals are noisy or unavailable, energy_per_ton calculation may be inaccurate, undermining operator feedback quality.
RISK: If Safety Services constraints are overly restrictive, they may prevent operators from achieving E_target even with optimal control.
RISK: Teleoperation latency may delay operator response to efficiency feedback, reducing effectiveness in dynamic worksite conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify in DP-C the specific safety constraints that Safety Services will enforce (e.g., maximum power draw limits, prohibited operational modes) and ensure they are compatible with E_target achievement. Define telemetry update frequency and latency requirements to ensure operator feedback is actionable.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns the energy monitoring, efficiency calculation, and control optimization logic. It measures power draw from propulsion and auxiliary subsystems, calculates energy_per_ton in real-time, and adjusts operational parameters (motor speed, load distribution, transmission mode) to optimize efficiency. Vehicle also enforces safety constraints received from Safety Services and reports telemetry (energy consumption, efficiency KPI, operational state) to Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits real-time energy consumption (kWh), tonnage moved (tons), calculated energy_per_ton (kWh/ton), efficiency status (on-target, warning, critical), and optimization recommendations to Teleoperation and Site Manager dashboards.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives operational mode commands (e.g., 'eco mode', 'standard mode', 'power mode'), load strategy directives (e.g., 'minimize energy', 'maximize throughput'), and task parameters (destination, load weight) from Teleoperation or direct operator input.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints and efficiency-related safety rules from Safety Services (e.g., 'do not exceed 150 kW', 'disable eco mode on slopes > 15%'). Vehicle reports efficiency-related safety events (e.g., 'energy limit exceeded', 'unsafe efficiency mode attempted') to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses Network Link to communicate Telemetry and SafetyAlert messages to Teleoperation and Safety Services; receives Tasking and SafetyAlert commands via Network Link.

module_containment_scores.notes: Vehicle is the natural owner of energy monitoring and efficiency optimization logic. All power measurement, calculation, and control adjustment occur within vehicle subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry reporting and safety constraint enforcement cross module boundaries (via well-defined interfaces).

Teleoperation could host efficiency calculation, but this introduces latency (remote calculation), network dependency, and operator feedback delay. Efficiency optimization requires real-time vehicle control authority, which Teleoperation does not own. High diffusion risk if efficiency logic is split between Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Safety Services owns constraint definition and safety rule enforcement, but not real-time efficiency optimization. If Safety Services hosts efficiency calculation, it becomes a bottleneck for real-time control. Appropriate role: Safety Services defines and validates efficiency-related safety constraints; Vehicle enforces them.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own specific subsystem logic. Assigning energy efficiency DP to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all sub-modules without clear ownership. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency monitoring, calculation, and optimization logic is best contained within the Vehicle module because: (1) power draw measurement and control authority reside in vehicle subsystems; (2) real-time responsiveness is critical and cannot tolerate remote calculation latency; (3) Vehicle already owns propulsion and auxiliary control, making efficiency optimization a natural extension; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) provide clear boundaries for operator feedback and safety constraint integration.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle module becomes too complex (energy monitoring + propulsion control + safety constraint enforcement), it may exceed design capacity. Mitigation: decompose Vehicle into sub-modules (e.g., PowerManagement, EfficiencyOptimizer, SafetyEnforcer) in next decomposition level.

RISK: If Safety Services constraints are not clearly specified, Vehicle may implement conflicting efficiency logic. Mitigation: establish a formal interface contract between Vehicle and Safety Services defining constraint format, update frequency, and conflict resolution.

RISK: If Teleoperation feedback latency is high, operators may not respond quickly to efficiency alerts, reducing effectiveness. Mitigation: define maximum acceptable latency for Telemetry updates (e.g., < 500 ms) and validate network performance.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Vehicle module has sufficient design capacity and expertise to own energy monitoring and efficiency optimization. Establish a formal interface specification between Vehicle and Safety Services defining efficiency-related safety constraints (format, semantics, update protocol). Define Telemetry update frequency and latency requirements to ensure operator feedback is actionable in real-time.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid centralized-distributed energy management) directly addresses all sub-FRs: (1) Vehicle-resident monitoring satisfies CR-NGHE-003.1 (continuous measurement and reporting); (2) Vehicle control optimization satisfies CR-NGHE-003.2 (control mechanisms); (3) Teleoperation feedback loop satisfies CR-NGHE-003.3 (real-time feedback); (4) Safety Services integration satisfies CR-NGHE-003.4 (safety and operational constraints). No independence violations detected; DP-C is a coherent solution concept.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Energy monitoring logic is contained in Vehicle (score 0.95), but telemetry reporting and safety constraint enforcement cross module boundaries via Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interfaces. This is acceptable because these are explicit, well-defined interfaces.

DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Efficiency optimization control authority is split between Vehicle (real-time adjustment) and Teleoperation (operator mode selection). This is acceptable because Vehicle owns the fast loop (millisecond-scale control) and Teleoperation owns the slow loop (operator decision-making).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves high containment (0.95) for energy efficiency DP. The 0.05 diffusion score reflects only necessary cross-module communication (Telemetry out, Tasking in, SafetyAlert inout). No problematic logic duplication or scattered ownership detected. Recommended: proceed with Vehicle as IM boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-003 can be verified by: (1) instrumenting Vehicle to measure energy_per_ton over an evaluation period; (2) comparing measured energy_per_ton against E_target; (3) validating that Teleoperation feedback is delivered within acceptable latency; (4) confirming that Safety Services constraints do not prevent E_target achievement. All verification activities are feasible within Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle owns energy monitoring, calculation, and optimization logic. Teleoperation owns operator feedback and mode selection. Safety Services owns constraint definition and validation. No ambiguity in responsibility boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Establish a formal interface specification between Vehicle and Safety Services defining efficiency-related safety constraints (e.g., maximum power draw, prohibited operational modes, slope-dependent restrictions). Include constraint format, update frequency, and conflict resolution protocol.
Define Telemetry update frequency and maximum acceptable latency (e.g., energy_per_ton updates every 100 ms, latency < 500 ms) to ensure operator feedback is actionable in real-time.

Clarify the definition of 'tonnage moved' for energy_per_ton calculation: does it include partial loads, multiple trips, or only completed deliveries? Ensure consistency across Vehicle measurement and Teleoperation reporting.

Plan next decomposition level to break down Vehicle module into sub-modules (e.g., PowerManagement, EfficiencyOptimizer, SafetyEnforcer) to manage complexity and ensure clear ownership of energy efficiency logic.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 has been successfully refined into four atomic sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-003.1 through 003.4), each addressing a distinct aspect of energy efficiency (measurement, control, feedback, safety integration). DP-C provides a coherent solution concept with clear module assignment (Vehicle as primary owner, Safety Services and Teleoperation as supporting modules). All gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with acceptable risk profiles. The next level of decomposition is necessary to detail how Vehicle module will implement energy monitoring, efficiency calculation, and control optimization logic, and to specify the formal interface contracts with Safety Services and Teleoperation.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and report energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tonnage moved) to enable real-time and post-hoc energy efficiency calculation.

CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall provide control mechanisms (power management, load optimization, operational mode selection) that allow operators and tele-operators to influence energy consumption during task execution.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall deliver real-time energy efficiency feedback (energy_per_ton KPI, efficiency alerts, optimization recommendations) to operators and tele-operators to support decision-making.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain energy efficiency performance within safety and operational constraints (worksite safety plan, equipment limits, task requirements).

assumptions: Vehicle subsystems (motor, hydraulics, transmission) provide measurable power draw signals or can be instrumented to do so.
E_target is defined by customer and provided as a configuration parameter to the NGHE solution.
Teleoperation network provides sufficient bandwidth and latency performance to deliver real-time energy efficiency feedback (< 500 ms latency).
Safety Services can define and enforce efficiency-related safety constraints without creating conflicts with E_target achievement.
'Tonnage moved' is consistently defined across Vehicle measurement and Teleoperation reporting (e.g., only completed deliveries, not partial loads).
open_risks: RISK: If vehicle power draw signals are noisy or unavailable, energy_per_ton calculation may be inaccurate, undermining operator feedback quality. Mitigation: validate power measurement accuracy during Vehicle design phase.

RISK: If Safety Services constraints are overly restrictive, they may prevent operators from achieving E_target even with optimal control. Mitigation: establish formal interface contract and conduct trade-off analysis during next decomposition level.

RISK: Teleoperation latency may delay operator response to efficiency feedback, reducing effectiveness in dynamic worksite conditions. Mitigation: define and validate maximum acceptable latency (e.g., < 500 ms) during Teleoperation design phase.

RISK: Vehicle module may become too complex if energy monitoring, efficiency optimization, and safety constraint enforcement are all implemented in a single module. Mitigation: plan sub-module decomposition (PowerManagement, EfficiencyOptimizer, SafetyEnforcer) in next level.
evidence_needed: Formal interface specification between Vehicle and Safety Services defining efficiency-related safety constraints (format, semantics, update protocol).
Telemetry update frequency and maximum acceptable latency requirements (e.g., energy_per_ton updates every 100 ms, latency < 500 ms).
Definition of 'tonnage moved' for energy_per_ton calculation (e.g., only completed deliveries, including partial loads, multiple trips).
Vehicle power measurement accuracy specification (e.g., ± 5% of actual power draw).
Network performance validation (bandwidth, latency) to support real-time Telemetry and Tasking communication.
Safety Services constraint definition and validation protocol (e.g., how constraints are updated, how conflicts are resolved).
